title: Deadlocks and Synchronisation
description: Covers the four deadlock conditions, the Banker's algorithm, critical sections, and classic synchronisation problems.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
Match each synchronisation issue to its description.
right: A lower-priority process holds a lock needed by a higher-priority one
Two or more processes wait indefinitely for events only caused by each other
A process waits indefinitely while others make progress

questions.prompt: Which classic algorithm grants a resource request only if the resulting state is safe, using declared maximum needs of each process?
What is the name for a region of code in which shared variables are accessed and which must execute with mutual exclusion?
Which of the following are properties an ideal synchronisation solution must satisfy?
Which of the following are TRUE about semaphores?
options: wait() decrements the value and blocks if the result is negative
A semaphore can only ever take the values 0 and 1
Can be used as a counting resource (for example, number of empty slots in a buffer)
A binary semaphore behaves similarly to a mutex lock

questions.prompt: Match each classic synchronisation problem to its core concern.
right: Avoiding circular wait when multiple processes grab paired resources
Letting many readers share access, but only one writer at a time
Producers and consumers sharing a fixed-capacity queue

questions.prompt: Which of the following are issues with Peterson's algorithm?
options: It can fail on modern architectures where read/write operations are reordered
It does not satisfy mutual exclusion
It cannot be generalised beyond two processes without modification
